A Lissajous curve painter would create those mesmerizing overlapping patterns as the ratios shift, and the watercolor bleeding would soften everything into organic washes. Particle flow with noise-based wandering could generate wind-swept effects, or I could try a generative bloom that branches outward like a plant, creating dendritic patterns. There's also reaction-diffusion simulation, though that might be computationally heavy. Calligraphy with random text is interesting but complex to implement well, and I could explore mandala-style sacred geometry as a more structured alternative to the kaleidoscope.\n\nBeyond those, I'm eyeing confetti bursts, vertical drips like rain paintings, bouncing physics particles, spirograph patterns from rolling circles, Pollock-style chaotic splatters, wave interference patterns, and boid-based flocking behavior. I need to narrow down to a few that feel visually distinct from each other and from what the kaleidoscope already does.\n\nI'm settling on three: Lissajous curves that morph and layer into complex shapes with soft pigment mixing, spirograph patterns from hypocycloids that create petal and star formations, and flocking particles that swarm together while painting, where each particle steers toward neighbors while maintaining distance to form emergent blob-like shapes.\n\nActually, I want to push the experimental angle further. Glitch art with chaotic jumps and color inversions, reaction-diffusion patterns that create organic leopard spots and coral formations, and flow fields where particles follow vector directions to produce wind-blown effects all feel more surprising and better showcase the watercolor properties like bleed and mixing.\n\nI'm committing to kaleidoscope as the base, then Lissajous curves as the second visualization. I'm exploring a few more generative approaches. A flow field driven by Perlin noise would let particles trace organic, topographic-like patterns. Then there's the idea of concentric pulses expanding and bleeding into each other, or wandering agents that create abstract scribbles. I'm trying to figure out which would be most visually distinct alongside the kaleidoscope effect.\n\nSo I'm settling on four modes: kaleidoscope with radial symmetry, Lissajous curves that slowly morph into complex shapes, a noise-based flow field for asymmetric wandering lines, and expanding pulse rings that interfere with each other. Each one has a different visual character.\n\nNow I'm thinking about how to incorporate text input into this. The user wants a way to visualize what they're typing, which opens up some interesting possibilities.\n\nI could let them type text and have it painted as watercolor letters on the canvas\u2014each character rendered with that natural bleed and granulation effect. Or I could tie each character to some painting behavior that changes color or shape. But the simplest approach is probably just to treat text as another visualization mode: user enters a word or phrase, clicks paint, and it gets rendered in watercolor style on the canvas.\n\nFor implementation, I'd add a text input field with a paint button, then when clicked, render the text to a hidden canvas using the browser's font rendering, sample that, and paint it with the watercolor brush.\n\nNow I'm thinking through the specifics: I'd create a `paintText` function that generates an offscreen canvas, draws the text with `fillText`, scans for which pixels contain ink, then calls `paintAt` for each one so the watercolor simulation handles the bleeding and color mixing. For the UX, I'll keep it simple\u2014place the text at the canvas center in a configurable font size rather than making users specify coordinates.\n\nThe trickier part is how the pigment selection interacts with the text. If the user has a pigment like Cerulean selected, the text should use that color. But with Rainbow selected, since each `paintAt` call happens at the same moment in time, all the ink pixels would get the same rainbow tint rather than varying across the text.\n\nFor the actual sampling approach, I'm thinking: render the text at a reasonable size like 80 pixels tall, sample only pixels above an alpha threshold to capture antialiasing without noise, then use a smaller resolution for each `paintAt` call to keep the number of calls manageable. Now I need to figure out the right granularity\u2014if the text is 200 pixels wide by 80 pixels tall, I need to decide how densely to sample it.\n\nThe issue is that sampling every pixel could mean thousands of `paintAt` calls, which gets slow fast. A better approach is to sample every Nth pixel\u2014like every 4th or 8th pixel in both directions\u2014which would bring it down to around 1000 calls or fewer. Or I could render the text to an offscreen canvas at the final size and iterate through the pixels, painting only where there's ink. Defining the paintText API...\n\nNow I'm setting up an offscreen canvas to render the text, measuring its dimensions, and then drawing it in black so I can sample the pixel data to find where the letters actually are. Writing the sampling loop... I'm overcomplicating this \u2014 let me just map offscreen pixels directly to grid cells at a 1:1 ratio, so the text size in grid units matches the pixel dimensions of the offscreen canvas. With a 200-pixel-wide text on a 1152-cell-wide grid, that's about 17% of the canvas width, which feels right.\n\nNow I need to decide where this text painting feature lives in the UI \u2014 whether it should be a control in the main panel or its own visualization mode, since it's a one-shot action rather than continuous rendering. I'll start simple with a text input field and paint button, letting users apply their selected brush\u2014including special effects like Rainbow or Mask\u2014to render text on the canvas. Building the UI component and event handlers...\n\nNow I'm thinking through font sizing to make text look natural on the canvas. At 80px font size on a 1920x1080 canvas, that gives me about 7% of the width per character, which feels reasonable for readability. I should probably make this adjustable or find a sweet spot that works well by default.\n\nFor positioning, I could let users click to place text, but that adds complexity. Centering the text on the canvas is simpler and cleaner. I'll go with that approach.\n\nOn font choice, I'm leaning toward bold sans-serif to give it a modern feel while still working with the watercolor aesthetic. It's cleaner than serif and has good presence without being too ornate.\n\nNow I'm mapping out the implementation: updating to v0.16, removing the radial-sweep phase from the kaleidoscope effect, adding three new visualizations\u2014lissajous curves, a flow field, and a pulse effect\u2014plus the text input UI and painting function. Then I'll wire it all together and test the behavior.\n\nStarting with the lissajous visualization...\n\nWriting lissajous implementation... I'm thinking through how Lissajous curves work\u2014they're defined by parametric equations where x and y oscillate at different frequencies, creating closed loops when the frequency ratio is rational. For this visualization, I'd center the curve on the canvas and let the frequency parameters morph slowly over time to create evolving patterns. I could also introduce randomness to add visual interest, or explore a flow field approach where particles follow directional vectors across the canvas and paint their paths.\n\nFor the flow field, I'd generate the vector directions using a noise-like function\u2014since we don't have Perlin noise available, I can approximate it with combinations of sine and cosine functions that vary by position and time. Actually, I could leverage the existing paper texture noise we already have as the underlying field. Now I'm setting up the flow field step function to initialize and update particle positions based on these directional vectors.\n\nImplementing the particle system... Finishing the particle painting and then spawning new ones to maintain a steady population. Now I'm thinking about a pulse mechanic where epicenters spawn randomly and expand outward as rings...\n\nWriting pulse simulation... Or fireworks with particle bursts and color trails radiating outward from points. Fireworks would definitely be visually striking, but I'm realizing the thunderstorm preset already has lightning, and the rain and snow effects already cover particle-based animations pretty well.       "description": "Add Lissajous, FlowField, Pulse + extend dispatcher and clear function",
      "new_str": "// --- Lissajous (v0.16) ---------------------------------------------------\n// A single master point traces  x(t) = sin(a\u00b7t + \u03c6x)\u00b7Rx,  y(t) = sin(b\u00b7t)\u00b7Ry\n// where (a, b, \u03c6x, Rx, Ry) all morph slowly via low-frequency carrier sines.\n// When the ratio a/b is rational, the figure is closed; irrational and it\n// fills the rectangle densely. Slowly drifting (a, b) means the figure\n// continuously morphs through different Lissajous topologies \u2014 nodes,\n// figure-eights, ellipses, complex weaves \u2014 building dense overlapping\n// curves on the canvas. Pigment cycles every ~600 frames so successive\n// figure-eras paint in different colors and KM-mix at their crossings.\n\nlet lissajousState = null;\n\nfunction lissajousStep() {\n  if (!lissajousState) {\n    lissajousState = { t: 0, pigment: 0, pigCycleAt: 0 };\n  }\n  const s = lissajousState;\n  s.t++;\n\n  // Carrier sines for slowly morphing (a, b). The ranges hold a and b\n  // in [1.2, 5.5] \u2014 too small a ratio (a/b near 1) collapses the figure\n  // to an ellipse; too large makes it scribble unrecognizably. Center\n  // values 3.5 and 2.6 are non-commensurate, so the morph never returns\n  // to the same configuration.\n  const a   = 3.5 + Math.sin(s.t * 0.00040)         * 2.0;\n  const b   = 2.6 + Math.sin(s.t * 0.00057 + 1.3)   * 1.8;\n  const phi = Math.PI * 0.5 + Math.sin(s.t * 0.0011) * 1.2;\n\n  // Position. Slightly larger horizontal radius matches typical\n  // landscape-orientation canvas aspect.\n  const cx = GW * 0.5;\n  const cy = GH * 0.5;\n  const Rx = GW * 0.40;\n  const Ry = GH * 0.42;\n  const x = cx + Math.sin(s.t * 0.026 * a / 3.5 + phi) * Rx;\n  const y = cy + Math.sin(s.t * 0.026 * b / 2.6)       * Ry;\n\n  // Pigment rotation. Switch to next pigment every 600 frames (~10s)\n  // so each figure-era leaves a distinct color trace.\n  if (s.t - s.pigCycleAt >= 600) {\n    s.pigment = (s.pigment + 1) % 3;\n    s.pigCycleAt = s.t;\n  }\n\n  const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();\n  const pxToGridRadius = (GW / rect.width) * 0.5;\n  paintAt(x, y, 11 * pxToGridRadius, s.pigment, 0.26);\n}\n\n// --- Flow field (v0.16) --------------------------------------------------\n// A bank of particles wanders through a vector field where each cell's\n// angle is determined by the existing paperH noise array + a slow time\n// shift. paperH is already a smooth noise field across the canvas\n// (used for granulation), so re-using it gives spatially-coherent flow\n// without needing a Perlin/simplex import.\n//\n// Particles spawn at random positions, follow the field for their\n// lifespan (150-350 frames), paint a small dab at each step, and die\n// off-canvas or on age-out. The pool refills continuously so the canvas\n// stays alive. Visually: wind-blown / topographic line patterns that\n// trace contour lines of the underlying noise field.\n\nconst FLOW_FIELD_PARTICLES = 22;\nlet flowFieldState = null;\n\nfunction flowFieldStep() {\n  if (!flowFieldState) flowFieldState = { particles: [], t: 0 };\n  const s = flowFieldState;\n  s.t++;\n\n  const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();\n  const pxToGridRadius = (GW / rect.width) * 0.5;\n\n  // Update existing particles\n  for (let i = s.particles.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) {\n    const p = s.particles[i];\n    p.age++;\n    if (p.age > p.maxAge || p.x < 0 || p.x >= GW || p.y < 0 || p.y >= GH) {\n      s.particles.splice(i, 1);\n      continue;\n    }\n    // Sample the paperH noise field at the particle's cell to get an\n    // angle. paperH values are roughly [0, 1] so multiplying by 4\u03c0\n    // gives the angle space two full turns of variation, which makes\n    // the flow lines curve more interestingly than just one turn.\n    // Time shift slowly rotates the whole field so the patterns\n    // morph as the wash continues.\n    const idx = Math.floor(p.y) * GW + Math.floor(p.x);\n    const noise = paperH[idx] || 0.5;\n    const angle = noise * Math.PI * 4 + s.t * 0.0006;\n    p.x += Math.cos(angle) * 2.0;\n    p.y += Math.sin(angle) * 2.0;\n    paintAt(p.x, p.y, 7 * pxToGridRadius, p.pigment, 0.20);\n  }\n\n  // Refill the pool so the canvas stays animated indefinitely\n  while (s.particles.length < FLOW_FIELD_PARTICLES) {\n    s.particles.push({\n      x: Math.random() * GW,\n      y: Math.random() * GH,\n      age: 0,\n      maxAge: 150 + Math.random() * 200,\n      pigment: Math.floor(Math.random() * 3),\n    });\n  }\n}\n\n// --- Pulse (v0.16) -------------------------------------------------------\n// Concentric expanding rings spawn at random epicenters, each painting\n// a circle of dabs at its growing radius. When a ring reaches max size,\n// it dies. Multiple rings co-exist; their painted regions overlap and\n// interfere via the watercolor sim, turning into organic blobs.\n//\n// Each pulse picks a random pigment so a session shows all three colors\n// crossing \u2014 KM-mixing where they overlap. Spawn rate jitters so pulses\n// don't pop on a fixed metronome.\n\nlet pulseState = null;\n\nfunction pulseStep() {\n  if (!pulseState) {\n    pulseState = { pulses: [], t: 0, lastSpawn: -100, nextSpawnGap: 60 };\n  }\n  const s = pulseState;\n  s.t++;\n\n  const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();\n  const pxToGridRadius = (GW / rect.width) * 0.5;\n\n  // Spawn new pulse on a jittered cadence (60-120 frames apart)\n  if (s.t - s.lastSpawn >= s.nextSpawnGap) {\n    s.pulses.push({\n      x: GW * (0.15 + Math.random() * 0.70),\n      y: GH * (0.15 + Math.random() * 0.70),\n      r: 0,\n      maxR: GH * (0.18 + Math.random() * 0.28),\n      pigment: Math.floor(Math.random() * 3),\n    });\n    s.lastSpawn = s.t;\n    s.nextSpawnGap = 60 + Math.floor(Math.random() * 60);\n  }\n\n  // Advance each pulse and paint its ring at the current radius\n  const N_POINTS = 32;  // sample density along the ring perimeter\n  for (let i = s.pulses.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) {\n    const p = s.pulses[i];\n    p.r += 1.6;\n    if (p.r > p.maxR) { s.pulses.splice(i, 1); continue; }\n    // Strength tapers as the ring grows \u2014 distant rings deposit less\n    // pigment, giving the visual sense of a fading wave-front rather\n    // than a constant-thickness expanding paint band.\n    const tapered = 1 - (p.r / p.maxR);\n    const strength = 0.18 + 0.20 * tapered;\n    for (let j = 0; j < N_POINTS; j++) {\n      const angle = (j / N_POINTS) * Math.PI * 2;\n      const x = p.x + Math.cos(angle) * p.r;\n      const y = p.y + Math.sin(angle) * p.r;\n      if (x < 0 || x >= GW || y < 0 || y >= GH) continue;\n      paintAt(x, y, 7 * pxToGridRadius, p.pigment, strength);\n    }\n  }\n}\n\n// ----- dispatcher -------------------------------------------------------\nfunction visualizationStep() {\n  switch (visualizationMode) {\n    case 'kaleidoscope': kaleidoscopeStep(); break;\n    case 'lissajous':    lissajousStep();    break;\n    case 'flowField':    flowFieldStep();    break;\n    case 'pulse':        pulseStep();        break;\n    // 'off' falls through to no-op\n  }\n}\n\nfunction clearAllVisualizationState() {\n  kaleidoscopeState = null;\n  lissajousState = null;\n  flowFieldState = null;\n  pulseState = null;\n}",
      "old_str": "// ----- dispatcher -------------------------------------------------------\nfunction visualizationStep() {\n  switch (visualizationMode) {\n    case 'kaleidoscope': kaleidoscopeStep(); break;\n    // 'off' falls through to no-op\n  }\n}\n\nfunction clearAllVisualizationState() {\n  kaleidoscopeState = null;\n}",

    "input": {
      "description": "Add paintText function + UI wiring",
      "new_str": "// ----- UI: visualization mode dropdown ----------------------------------\nconst visualizationSelect = document.getElementById('visualization-mode');\nvisualizationSelect.addEventListener('change', () => {\n  clearAllVisualizationState();\n  visualizationMode = visualizationSelect.value;\n});\n\n// ============================================================\n// TEXT PAINTING (v0.16)\n// ============================================================\n// Renders user-entered text into a hidden offscreen canvas using the\n// browser's font engine, then samples that bitmap to drive a flurry of\n// paintAt calls \u2014 one per ink pixel above an alpha threshold. The\n// watercolor sim picks up from there: each dab bleeds via the wet\n// substrate, edges darken, neighboring strokes KM-mix.\n//\n// Uses currentPigment for the deposit, so the text honors whatever\n// brush the user has active \u2014 type a phrase, switch to Rainbow, paint\n// it. Switch to Mask, paint it, paint a wash, click Remove mask to\n// reveal the text as paper-white. Switch to Water to soften an\n// already-painted area in a text shape. The whole palette composes.\n\n// Configuration. Font size is measured in offscreen-canvas pixels, which\n// we treat 1:1 with grid cells (so an 80px font produces text 80 grid\n// cells tall \u2014 about 12% of canvas height at default resolution). Sample\n// step controls how densely the bitmap is read: smaller = denser dots\n// per letter (more paintAt calls, denser paint), larger = sparser stamps\n// that let the watercolor bleed do more interpolation work.\nconst TEXT_FONT_SIZE = 96;\nconst TEXT_SAMPLE_STEP = 4;\nconst TEXT_PAINT_STRENGTH = 0.40;\n\nfunction paintText(text, opts) {\n  opts = opts || {};\n  const fontSize = opts.fontSize || TEXT_FONT_SIZE;\n  const sampleStep = opts.sampleStep || TEXT_SAMPLE_STEP;\n  const strength = opts.strength || TEXT_PAINT_STRENGTH;\n  const pigment = opts.pigment !== undefined ? opts.pigment : currentPigment;\n  // Center of canvas by default; opts.x / opts.y override in grid cells.\n  const centerGX = opts.x !== undefined ? opts.x : GW * 0.5;\n  const centerGY = opts.y !== undefined ? opts.y : GH * 0.5;\n\n  // Render text into an offscreen canvas. Use a heavy serif weight so\n  // the strokes are thick enough to sample densely \u2014 thin letterforms\n  // would sample too few ink pixels and read as a string of disconnected\n  // dots through the watercolor bleed.\n  const tmp = document.createElement('canvas');\n  const tctx = tmp.getContext('2d');\n  const fontSpec = `bold ${fontSize}px Georgia, serif`;\n  tctx.font = fontSpec;\n  const metrics = tctx.measureText(text);\n  const padX = Math.ceil(fontSize * 0.1);\n  const padY = Math.ceil(fontSize * 0.1);\n  const w = Math.max(1, Math.ceil(metrics.width) + padX * 2);\n  const h = Math.ceil(fontSize * 1.4) + padY * 2;\n  tmp.width = w;\n  tmp.height = h;\n  // Re-set font; the canvas resize wiped the context state.\n  tctx.font = fontSpec;\n  tctx.textBaseline = 'middle';\n  tctx.fillStyle = '#000';\n  tctx.fillText(text, padX, h * 0.5);\n\n  // Sample the bitmap. For each ink pixel (alpha > threshold), drop a\n  // paintAt at the corresponding grid position. Offscreen canvas\n  // coordinates are treated as grid cells 1:1 (no rescale) \u2014 so font\n  // size directly maps to letter height in grid cells. The text is\n  // centered at (centerGX, centerGY).\n  const img = tctx.getImageData(0, 0, w, h).data;\n  const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();\n  const pxToGridRadius = (GW / rect.width) * 0.5;\n  // Brush radius slightly larger than half the sample step so adjacent\n  // dabs overlap \u2014 letters read as filled strokes rather than dot grids.\n  const brushPx = sampleStep * 1.6;\n\n  // Mark canvas active so the simulation wakes up and starts spreading\n  // the text via wet diffusion / advection as we paint each dab.\n  markCanvasActive();\n\n  const startX = centerGX - w * 0.5;\n  const startY = centerGY - h * 0.5;\n  let painted = 0;\n  for (let py = 0; py < h; py += sampleStep) {\n    for (let px = 0; px < w; px += sampleStep) {\n      const idx = (py * w + px) * 4;\n      if (img[idx + 3] < 128) continue;   // not enough ink at this pixel\n      const gx = startX + px;\n      const gy = startY + py;\n      if (gx < 0 || gx >= GW || gy < 0 || gy >= GH) continue;\n      paintAt(gx, gy, brushPx * pxToGridRadius, pigment, strength);\n      painted++;\n    }\n  }\n  return painted;  // for testing / diagnostics\n}\n\n// Wire the input + button. Enter in the input also fires Paint, so the\n// flow is type \u2192 Enter without hunting for the button.\nconst textInputField = document.getElementById('text-input');\nconst textPaintBtn = document.getElementById('text-paint-btn');\nfunction doTextPaint() {\n  const t = textInputField.value;\n  if (!t) return;\n  paintText(t);\n  textPaintBtn.classList.add('on');\n  setTimeout(() => textPaintBtn.classList.remove('on'), 220);\n}\ntextPaintBtn.addEventListener('click', doTextPaint);\ntextInputField.addEventListener('keydown', (e) => {\n  if (e.key === 'Enter') { e.preventDefault(); doTextPaint(); }\n});",

When the test calls paintText directly with an empty string, it bypasses that check and returns 440 dabs instead of handling it properly.\n\nThe root cause is that our mock for getImageData doesn't distinguish between actual content and empty input \u2014 it just returns a fake pattern regardless of what was drawn. In a real browser, fillText with an empty string would draw nothing and getImageData would return transparent pixels. I should add a guard at the start of paintText to handle empty input directly, making it robust regardless of how it's called.\n\nEverything else is working as expected \u2014 the visualizations render, the particle pools fill correctly, and the text painting produces the right number of dabs for actual content.     "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.16 \u2014 three new visualizations + text input**\n\n**Kaleidoscope:** removed radial-sweep. Now cycles arc-trace \u2192 dot-burst \u2192 rose-petals.\n\n**Three new visualizations:**\n\n**Lissajous** \u2014 a single master point traces `x = sin(a\u00b7t + \u03c6)\u00b7Rx`, `y = sin(b\u00b7t)\u00b7Ry` where `a`, `b`, and `\u03c6` morph slowly via low-frequency carrier sines (`0.0004 Hz` etc.). The center values `a\u22483.5`, `b\u22482.6` are non-commensurate, so the morph never returns to the same configuration \u2014 the figure passes through ellipses, figure-eights, nodes, woven knots over minutes. Pigment cycles every 600 frames so each era of curves paints in a different color and KM-mixes at their crossings.\n\n**Flow Field** \u2014 22 particles wander through a vector field built from the existing `paperH` noise array + a slow time shift (`s.t * 0.0006`). Each frame: sample `paperH[i] * 4\u03c0 + t\u00b7offset` for the angle, advance the particle by 2 cells along that angle, paint a small dab. Particles die at 150\u2013350 frames or off-canvas, and the pool refills continuously. Reusing `paperH` (the granulation noise) gives spatially-coherent flow lines without importing Perlin \u2014 they trace contour-like patterns that morph slowly as the time offset rotates the field.\n\n**Pulse** \u2014 concentric rings expand from random epicenters with jittered spawn cadence (60\u2013120 frames). Each ring draws 32 dab-points along its perimeter, with strength tapering as the ring grows (`0.18 + 0.20 * (1 - r/maxR)`) so distant rings deposit less pigment \u2014 visually reads as fading wave-fronts rather than constant-thickness paint bands. Each pulse picks a random pigment so the canvas accumulates rose / yellow / cerulean wave-fronts that KM-mix at their overlaps.\n\n**Text painting** \u2014 new input field + \"Paint\" button below the Visualization dropdown. Press Enter or click Paint to render the entered text:\n\n1. Offscreen canvas, bold Georgia at 96px, measure text width\n2. Sample the bitmap every 4 px; cells with alpha \u2265 128 are \"ink\"\n3. For each ink cell, call `paintAt` at the corresponding grid position with brush radius slightly larger than the sample step (so dabs overlap into filled strokes rather than dot grids)\n\nThe function uses `currentPigment`, so text honors whatever brush you have active. Some combos worth trying:\n- **Rainbow** brush + Text \u2192 multi-color text that shifts hue across the stroke\n- **Mask** brush + Text \u2192 mask out a text shape, paint a wash over it, click \"Remove mask\" \u2192 reveal paper-white text\n- **Water** brush + Text \u2192 softens an already-painted region into the text shape (a \"wash-out\" effect)\n- **Cerulean + Auto-dry background + Night** \u2192 write a poem across a deep night sky\n\nThe implementation is one function (`paintText(text, opts)`) \u2014 `opts` accepts `x`, `y`, `fontSize`, `sampleStep`, `strength`, `pigment` overrides for programmatic use.",

    "thinking": " The user wants to trace SVG files onto the watercolor canvas, similar to how text painting works. I'm considering two approaches: either converting the SVG to a bitmap and sampling pixels to paint, or actually following the SVG paths themselves with the brush strokes.\n\nPath tracing seems more compelling because it creates animation, feels more authentic to watercolor, and lets the simulation's bleed and edge effects shine through. I'm thinking the best solution would let users paste or upload SVG files, extract the paths, then animate a brush moving along each path while calling paintAt at intervals\u2014potentially running multiple paths in parallel or sequentially to create that natural drawing effect.\n\nFor implementation, I'd parse the SVG to find all drawable elements, convert them to point sequences using the browser's built-in path sampling methods (getTotalLength and getPointAtLength), then queue and animate through those points each frame. The alternative would be rendering the SVG to a canvas and sampling it like text, but that loses the animated tracing feel. The path-tracing approach is worth pursuing since it's more visually engaging and really demonstrates what the watercolor simulation can do. I'm realizing the conversion logic for different SVG element types is getting unwieldy, so I'll start by just handling `<path>` elements since most vector graphics export that way anyway.\n\nWriting conversion function... Writing SVG shape converters...\n\nNow I'm thinking through the UI flow: users can upload an SVG file or paste one, then hit a trace button to queue up the paths for animation. The system will progressively draw each path with the watercolor brush effect at a controlled pace, and I need to handle the SVG's positioning and scaling to fit the canvas properly.\n\nFor the implementation, I'm setting up state to track the current trace operation and the queue of paths being drawn.\n\nBuilding the step function... Still writing SVG parsing... Still writing the path sampling logic... Adjusting the sampling strategy...\n\nI need to recalibrate the sample density based on the scaling factor\u2014since SVG units map to grid cells through the `scale` parameter, I should sample at intervals that keep consecutive points roughly 2-3 cells apart, which means the interval in SVG units is `SAMPLE_DENSITY_CELLS / scale`. Then I can calculate the total number of samples needed by dividing the path length by this interval.\n\nFor the UI, I'm deciding between file upload, text paste, or a sample library. File input is the most straightforward since users typically have SVG files ready, though I could also support pasting SVG content directly.\n\nSetting up the file input...\n\nNow I'm thinking through the edge cases: SVGs without viewBox attributes should fall back to width and height, or default to 100x100 if neither exists. Empty SVGs with no paths are fine\u2014they just won't produce any painting. Large SVGs with many path points will take longer to trace, which is acceptable. The tricky part is handling transforms on group elements; I'll skip nested transforms for now and assume the SVG is relatively flat.\n\nWhen I use `getPointAtLength()` on a path element I create in a fresh temporary SVG, there are no parent transforms to worry about, so I can work directly with the path's `d` attribute without needing to apply complex transform logic.\n\nThe tricky part is that if the original SVG has transforms on parent groups, those get lost when I just extract the `d` attribute \u2014 a path inside a rotated group won't maintain that rotation. Properly handling this would require flattening all ancestor transforms, which is complex, so I'll document this as a limitation and move forward with the simpler approach that works for most SVGs.\n\nFor the animation itself, I need to make the trace happen progressively over time, painting some points per frame from the queue rather than all at once, so I need to figure out the right pace.\n\nI'm settling on sampling every 2 cells along the path and painting 8 points per frame, which gives about 16 cells of path per frame \u2014 for a typical 500-cell path that's roughly half a second of animation, fast enough to be visible but slow enough to let the watercolor effect develop between dabs.\n\nWriting the animation code... Writing the trace functions...\n\nI'm debating whether to pull stroke colors from the SVG itself and match them to the closest pigment in the palette, or just stick with the current brush color for simplicity. Reading colors and computing distance to each pigment would be nice, but it adds complexity\u2014I'll keep it simple for now and let users switch brushes if they want different colors.\n\nI'm also realizing that SVG paths might include filled regions, not just outlines. Tracing just the outline with the brush would leave the inside empty, which works for line drawings but not filled shapes. For v0.17, outline tracing is enough since the user asked to \"trace\"\u2014if they need filled shapes later, I could add a separate fill mode that rasterizes the SVG to a bitmap like the text approach does.\n\nLet me just implement trace mode and start coding.\n\nNow I'm thinking about where to place the SVG trace control in the panel\u2014right after the text input makes sense. I need to check what DOM methods are available for parsing SVG paths and sampling points along them, and make sure the test environment can mock those properly.\n\nI'll write the code first and handle testing afterward. For the user experience, I'll skip any special UI indicator and just let the canvas painting provide visual feedback that the trace completed.\n\nThe tricky part is that getTotalLength and getPointAtLength need a real DOM SVG element, which doesn't exist in Node.js tests. I'll need to either mock aggressively for tests or skip runtime testing altogether.\n\nFor testing, I'll keep it minimal \u2014 just verify the function exists and that the dispatcher calls it, without actually running the SVG parsing logic.\n\nNow I'm thinking through the parameters: a default brush radius of 5 grid cells, sample density of 2 cells between points to ensure overlap, paint strength around 0.35, and about 8-10 points per frame so a 200-sample path renders over 20-25 frames.\n\nLet me start writing the implementation.\n\nLooking at the main loop, I'll add the svgTraceStep right after visualizationStep so it runs each frame alongside the other updates.\n\nNow I'm weighing whether to support pasting SVG text directly into a textarea for quick snippets, versus requiring file uploads \u2014 the textarea approach is more convenient but file input is more standard for SVG workflows.\n\nI'll stick with file input only for this version and save the paste feature for later if it proves useful.\n\nMy implementation plan is locked: add a file input with a load button, create functions to parse the SVG and extract geometry into path strings, then call svgTraceStep each frame to paint the traced points incrementally.       "new_str": "textPaintBtn.addEventListener('click', doTextPaint);\ntextInputField.addEventListener('keydown', (e) => {\n  if (e.key === 'Enter') { e.preventDefault(); doTextPaint(); }\n});\n\n// ============================================================\n// SVG TRACING (v0.17)\n// ============================================================\n// User picks an SVG file; the parser walks every geometry element\n// (path / line / polyline / polygon / rect / circle / ellipse),\n// converts each to a path-d string, and uses the browser's built-in\n// SVGPathElement.getPointAtLength to sample arc-length-parametrized\n// points along it. That guarantees b\u00e9ziers, arcs, and other curves\n// trace at consistent visual spacing regardless of their parametric\n// speed \u2014 uniform brushstroke density along every curve.\n//\n// All sampled points are queued. Each frame paints SVG_POINTS_PER_FRAME\n// of them through paintAt, using whatever brush the user has active\n// (currentPigment). The user watches the SVG draw itself with the\n// watercolor brush, getting the bleed/edge/granulation features of the\n// sim along every stroke.\n//\n// Scale: SVG fits into ~70% of canvas, preserving aspect ratio, centered.\n//\n// Limitations: parent-group transforms (e.g. <g transform=\"rotate()\">)\n// are not applied \u2014 paths are read from their raw `d` attributes. SVGs\n// authored without nested transforms (typical exports from vector tools)\n// trace correctly. Strokes/fills from the SVG itself aren't used; the\n// active brush determines the color/behavior.\n\nconst SVG_TRACE_FIT = 0.70;           // fraction of canvas the SVG fills\nconst SVG_POINTS_PER_FRAME = 10;      // trace speed\nconst SVG_SAMPLE_DENSITY_CELLS = 2.0; // sample every ~2 grid cells along each path\nconst SVG_TRACE_STRENGTH = 0.42;\nconst SVG_TRACE_BRUSH_PX = 9;\n\nlet svgTraceState = null;             // { points: [{x,y}], idx, pigment, brushPx, strength }\n\n// Convert any single SVG geometry element to an equivalent path \"d\"\n// string. Lets us treat lines/polygons/rects/circles/ellipses uniformly\n// through SVGPathElement.getPointAtLength.\nfunction pathStringFromSvgElement(el) {\n  const tag = el.tagName.toLowerCase();\n  const get = (n, def) => {\n    const v = el.getAttribute(n);\n    return v != null ? +v : (def !== undefined ? def : 0);\n  };\n  switch (tag) {\n    case 'path': return el.getAttribute('d');\n    case 'line':\n      return `M ${get('x1')} ${get('y1')} L ${get('x2')} ${get('y2')}`;\n    case 'polyline':\n    case 'polygon': {\n      const pts = (el.getAttribute('points') || '').trim().split(/[\\s,]+/);\n      if (pts.length < 4) return null;\n      let d = '';\n      for (let i = 0; i + 1 < pts.length; i += 2) {\n        d += (i === 0 ? 'M ' : 'L ') + pts[i] + ' ' + pts[i+1] + ' ';\n      }\n      if (tag === 'polygon') d += 'Z';\n      return d;\n    }\n    case 'rect': {\n      const x = get('x'), y = get('y'), w = get('width'), h = get('height');\n      if (w <= 0 || h <= 0) return null;\n      return `M ${x} ${y} H ${x+w} V ${y+h} H ${x} Z`;\n    }\n    case 'circle': {\n      const cx = get('cx'), cy = get('cy'), r = get('r');\n      if (r <= 0) return null;\n      // Two half-arcs form a full circle. SVG-path arc commands take\n      // rx, ry, x-axis-rotation, large-arc-flag, sweep-flag, x, y.\n      return `M ${cx-r} ${cy} A ${r} ${r} 0 1 0 ${cx+r} ${cy} A ${r} ${r} 0 1 0 ${cx-r} ${cy}`;\n    }\n    case 'ellipse': {\n      const cx = get('cx'), cy = get('cy'), rx = get('rx'), ry = get('ry');\n      if (rx <= 0 || ry <= 0) return null;\n      return `M ${cx-rx} ${cy} A ${rx} ${ry} 0 1 0 ${cx+rx} ${cy} A ${rx} ${ry} 0 1 0 ${cx-rx} ${cy}`;\n    }\n  }\n  return null;\n}\n\nfunction loadSVGAndTrace(svgText) {\n  // Parse the SVG without executing scripts. DOMParser image/svg+xml\n  // mode is safe for untrusted SVGs (no <script> execution, no fetch).\n  const parser = new DOMParser();\n  const doc = parser.parseFromString(svgText, 'image/svg+xml');\n  const root = doc.documentElement;\n  if (!root || root.tagName.toLowerCase() !== 'svg') {\n    console.warn('Trace SVG: not a valid SVG document');\n    return 0;\n  }\n\n  // Determine the SVG's coordinate frame. viewBox wins; otherwise\n  // width/height attributes; otherwise default 100x100.\n  let vbX = 0, vbY = 0, vbW = 100, vbH = 100;\n  const vbAttr = root.getAttribute('viewBox');\n  if (vbAttr) {\n    const parts = vbAttr.trim().split(/[\\s,]+/).map(Number);\n    if (parts.length === 4 && parts.every(n => isFinite(n))) {\n      vbX = parts[0]; vbY = parts[1]; vbW = parts[2]; vbH = parts[3];\n    }\n  } else {\n    const wAttr = +root.getAttribute('width');\n    const hAttr = +root.getAttribute('height');\n    if (isFinite(wAttr) && wAttr > 0) vbW = wAttr;\n    if (isFinite(hAttr) && hAttr > 0) vbH = hAttr;\n  }\n  if (vbW <= 0 || vbH <= 0) { vbW = 100; vbH = 100; }\n\n  // Fit SVG into SVG_TRACE_FIT * min(GW, GH). Preserve aspect ratio.\n  // Centered on the canvas \u2014 offsetX/Y move the (vbX, vbY) origin to\n  // the top-left corner of the centered destination rectangle.\n  const targetW = GW * SVG_TRACE_FIT;\n  const targetH = GH * SVG_TRACE_FIT;\n  const scale = Math.min(targetW / vbW, targetH / vbH);\n  const offsetX = GW * 0.5 - (vbW * scale) * 0.5;\n  const offsetY = GH * 0.5 - (vbH * scale) * 0.5;\n\n  // To call getTotalLength / getPointAtLength we need real SVGPathElements\n  // attached to a document. Build a hidden working SVG to host them.\n  const SVG_NS = 'http://www.w3.org/2000/svg';\n  const work = document.createElementNS(SVG_NS, 'svg');\n  work.setAttribute('width', '1');\n  work.setAttribute('height', '1');\n  work.style.position = 'absolute';\n  work.style.left = '-9999px';\n  work.style.opacity = '0';\n  document.body.appendChild(work);\n\n  const elements = doc.querySelectorAll(\n    'path, line, polyline, polygon, rect, circle, ellipse');\n  const points = [];\n\n  for (const el of elements) {\n    const dStr = pathStringFromSvgElement(el);\n    if (!dStr) continue;\n    const tmpPath = document.createElementNS(SVG_NS, 'path');\n    tmpPath.setAttribute('d', dStr);\n    work.appendChild(tmpPath);\n    let totalLen = 0;\n    try { totalLen = tmpPath.getTotalLength(); } catch (e) { /* unparseable d */ }\n    if (totalLen > 0) {\n      // Sample interval in SVG units = desired cells / scale. Density\n      // is in grid-cell space so brush dabs always overlap visually\n      // regardless of the SVG's native scale.\n      const intervalSVG = SVG_SAMPLE_DENSITY_CELLS / scale;\n      const numSamples = Math.max(2, Math.ceil(totalLen / intervalSVG));\n      for (let i = 0; i <= numSamples; i++) {\n        const len = (i / numSamples) * totalLen;\n        let pt;\n        try { pt = tmpPath.getPointAtLength(len); } catch (e) { continue; }\n        const gx = offsetX + (pt.x - vbX) * scale;\n        const gy = offsetY + (pt.y - vbY) * scale;\n        if (gx < 0 || gx >= GW || gy < 0 || gy >= GH) continue;\n        points.push({ x: gx, y: gy });\n      }\n    }\n    work.removeChild(tmpPath);\n  }\n  document.body.removeChild(work);\n\n  if (points.length === 0) {\n    console.warn('Trace SVG: no traceable geometry found');\n    return 0;\n  }\n\n  // Replace any in-flight trace \u2014 loading a new SVG cancels the previous.\n  const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();\n  const pxToGridRadius = (GW / rect.width) * 0.5;\n  svgTraceState = {\n    points,\n    idx: 0,\n    pigment: currentPigment,\n    brushRadius: SVG_TRACE_BRUSH_PX * pxToGridRadius,\n    strength: SVG_TRACE_STRENGTH,\n  };\n  return points.length;\n}\n\nfunction svgTraceStep() {\n  if (!svgTraceState) return;\n  const s = svgTraceState;\n  // Wake the canvas so simStep runs the bleed/granulation for each new\n  // dab as it's painted \u2014 keeps the trace feeling live.\n  markCanvasActive();\n  const end = Math.min(s.idx + SVG_POINTS_PER_FRAME, s.points.length);\n  for (let i = s.idx; i < end; i++) {\n    const p = s.points[i];\n    paintAt(p.x, p.y, s.brushRadius, s.pigment, s.strength);\n  }\n  s.idx = end;\n  if (s.idx >= s.points.length) svgTraceState = null;\n}\n\n// Wire the file picker. The visible button proxies to the hidden input\n// so the user clicks one familiar control rather than a default browser\n// file widget.\nconst svgFileInput = document.getElementById('svg-file-input');\nconst svgLoadBtn = document.getElementById('svg-load-btn');\nsvgLoadBtn.addEventListener('click', () => svgFileInput.click());\nsvgFileInput.addEventListener('change', async (e) => {\n  const file = e.target.files && e.target.files[0];\n  if (!file) return;\n  try {\n    const text = await file.text();\n    const n = loadSVGAndTrace(text);\n    if (n > 0) {\n      svgLoadBtn.classList.add('on');\n      setTimeout(() => svgLoadBtn.classList.remove('on'), 220);\n    }\n  } catch (err) {\n    console.warn('Trace SVG: failed to read file', err);\n  }\n  // Reset value so the same file can be re-loaded with a different brush.\n  e.target.value = '';\n});",

      "command": "cd /home/claude && cat > /tmp/v17.js << 'EOF'\n// Mock just enough DOM + SVGPathElement to verify the full trace pipeline.\nconst fs = require('fs');\nconst html = fs.readFileSync('watercolor.html', 'utf8');\nconst m = html.match(/<script>([\\s\\S]*?)<\\/script>/);\nconst src = m[1];\n\n// SVG path mock: builds an internal point list from the d-string and exposes\n// getTotalLength / getPointAtLength. Only handles M, L, H, V, Z, A (treated as L for testing)\n// \u2014 that's enough for our pathStringFromSvgElement output to be traceable.\nclass MockSVGPath {\n  constructor() { this._d = ''; this._pts = []; }\n  setAttribute(name, value) { if (name === 'd') { this._d = value; this._rebuild(); } }\n  getAttribute(name) { return name === 'd' ? this._d : null; }\n  _rebuild() {\n    const tokens = this._d.replace(/,/g, ' ').split(/\\s+/).filter(Boolean);\n    const pts = [];\n    let x = 0, y = 0, i = 0;\n    while (i < tokens.length) {\n      const cmd = tokens[i++];\n      const num = parseFloat;\n      switch (cmd) {\n        case 'M': x = num(tokens[i++]); y = num(tokens[i++]); pts.push([x, y]); break;\n        case 'L': x = num(tokens[i++]); y = num(tokens[i++]); pts.push([x, y]); break;\n        case 'H': x = num(tokens[i++]);                       pts.push([x, y]); break;\n        case 'V': y = num(tokens[i++]);                       pts.push([x, y]); break;\n        case 'A': // skip rx,ry,xar,laf,sf \u2014 then x,y\n          i += 5; x = num(tokens[i++]); y = num(tokens[i++]); pts.push([x, y]); break;\n        case 'Z': if (pts.length > 0) pts.push(pts[0]); break;\n      }\n    }\n    this._pts = pts;\n  }\n  getTotalLength() {\n    let len = 0;\n    for (let j = 1; j < this._pts.length; j++) {\n      const dx = this._pts[j][0] - this._pts[j-1][0];\n      const dy = this._pts[j][1] - this._pts[j-1][1];\n      len += Math.hypot(dx, dy);\n    }\n    return len;\n  }\n  getPointAtLength(t) {\n    if (this._pts.length === 0) return { x: 0, y: 0 };\n    let len = 0;\n    for (let j = 1; j < this._pts.length; j++) {\n      const [x1, y1] = this._pts[j-1];\n      const [x2, y2] = this._pts[j];\n      const seg = Math.hypot(x2-x1, y2-y1);\n      if (t <= len + seg) {\n        const f = (t - len) / Math.max(seg, 1e-9);\n        return { x: x1 + (x2-x1) * f, y: y1 + (y2-y1) * f };\n      }\n      len += seg;\n    }\n    const last = this._pts[this._pts.length - 1];\n    return { x: last[0], y: last[1] };\n  }\n}\n\nconst els = {};\nfunction makeEl(id) {\n  if (id && els[id]) return els[id];\n  const e = { id, value: id === 'animation-mode' || id === 'visualization-mode' ? 'off' : (id === 'time-of-day' ?     "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.17 \u2014 SVG tracing**\n\nNew \"Trace SVG\" control below Text input. Click **Load SVG**, pick a file, watch a watercolor brush trace every stroke at ~10 points per frame.\n\n**The pipeline:**\n\n1. `DOMParser.parseFromString(text, 'image/svg+xml')` \u2014 safe parse, no script execution\n2. Read viewBox / width / height for the coordinate frame\n3. `querySelectorAll('path, line, polyline, polygon, rect, circle, ellipse')` \u2014 every geometry element\n4. `pathStringFromSvgElement(el)` normalizes each to a path `d` string:\n   - line \u2192 `M x1 y1 L x2 y2`\n   - rect \u2192 `M x y H x+w V y+h H x Z`\n   - circle \u2192 `M cx-r cy A r r 0 1 0 cx+r cy A r r 0 1 0 cx-r cy` (two half-arcs)\n   - polygon \u2192 polyline + `Z`\n5. Attach each to a hidden offscreen `<svg>`, call `SVGPathElement.getTotalLength()` for the arc-length total\n6. Sample every `SVG_SAMPLE_DENSITY_CELLS / scale` SVG units along the path via `getPointAtLength()`. This is the key for natural watercolor strokes: arc-length parametrization means brush dabs land at *uniform visual distances* even on curves where the parametric speed varies (b\u00e9zier handles, arc midpoints).\n7. Scale to fit 70% of canvas, center, push points into `svgTraceState.points`\n8. Each frame, `svgTraceStep` pops 10 points and `paintAt` them with `currentPigment`\n\n**Compositions worth trying:**\n\n- **Rainbow brush + SVG of a flower** \u2192 each stroke shifts hue as it traces, ending in a multi-color watercolor flower\n- **Mask brush + SVG of a tree silhouette \u2192 Background: Sunset \u2192 Remove mask** \u2192 a tree silhouette in negative-space against a sunset sky\n- **Water brush + SVG over an already-painted area** \u2192 softens the painted region into the SVG outline, like a \"wash-out\" decal\n- **Trace + Auto-dry background + Background: Night** \u2192 black-sky line drawing that dries into the night\n\n**Limitations:**\n- Parent-group transforms (`<g transform=\"\u2026\">`) aren't applied \u2014 paths read straight from their `d` attribute. SVGs exported \"flat\" from vector tools (Illustrator, Inkscape, Figma \u2014 most exports) trace correctly. If you have rotated/scaled groups, \"Flatten Transforms\" in your editor first.\n- The SVG's own stroke/fill colors are ignored \u2014 the active brush picks the color (this is the design: you choose how the SVG paints in watercolor).\n- Filled shapes paint as outlines only (the brush traces the boundary). Combine with overlapping strokes if you want fill effects.",
    "citations": []
  }

      "new_str": "  if (framesSinceBars >= BARS_TICK_INTERVAL) {\n    framesSinceBars = 0;\n    updateBars();\n  }\n  requestAnimationFrame(loop);\n}\nloop();\n\n// ============================================================\n// PROGRAMMATIC API (v0.18) \u2014 window.Watercolor\n// ============================================================\n// All controls available in the UI are exposed here as a flat namespace\n// so users can drive the simulation from the browser console, an\n// external <script>, or a separately-loaded automation file.\n//\n// DESIGN CHOICES:\n// \u2022 Cursors are pure data objects. They never auto-paint \u2014 the caller\n//   sets position via .move() and explicitly calls .stamp() to deposit.\n//   Continuous painting is achieved by setInterval/rAF in caller code.\n//   Rationale: leaves the timing/animation policy fully to the user;\n//   the API stays small and composable.\n//\n// \u2022 Animation/Visualization/Background each accept {replace: bool}.\n//   Default is true (mirror dropdown behavior \u2014 switching modes clears\n//   the previous one's state). With replace:false, new state co-exists\n//   with whatever is already running. Only Animation and Visualization\n//   have meaningful additive behavior; Background washes are one-shot\n//   anyway, so replace is ignored there.\n//\n// \u2022 Toggle setters take an explicit boolean. Calling .edgeDarkening()\n//   with no arg returns the current value (getter mode). This keeps\n//   the API symmetric for read+write without overloading method names.\n//\n// \u2022 All public names are lowercase-camelCase. The constructor returns a\n//   plain object; no this-binding required \u2014 callers can destructure\n//   without losing context.\n\n// --- Color/mode name \u2192 internal pigment index --------------------------\n// Maps the user-facing color names to the brush index constants the\n// sim uses internally. Negative indices are special brushes (water,\n// lift, mask, rainbow); 0-2 are the three real pigments.\nconst API_COLOR_TO_INDEX = {\n  rose: 0, yellow: 1, blue: 2, cerulean: 2,\n  rainbow: RAINBOW_INDEX,\n  water: WATER_INDEX,\n  lift: LIFT_INDEX,\n  mask: MASK_INDEX,\n};\n\nfunction resolveColorToIndex(name) {\n  const k = String(name || '').toLowerCase();\n  if (!(k in API_COLOR_TO_INDEX)) {\n    throw new Error(\n      'Watercolor: unknown color/mode \"' + name + '\". ' +\n      'Valid: rose, yellow, blue, rainbow, water, lift, mask');\n  }\n  return API_COLOR_TO_INDEX[k];\n}\n\n// --- Cursor handles ----------------------------------------------------\n// Each Watercolor.cursor() returns one of these. Cursors hold position\n// + appearance state. They paint only when .stamp() is called, so the\n// caller controls timing entirely (loop, on-event, one-off, etc.).\nconst apiCursors = new Set();   // for diagnostics / iteration\nlet apiNextCursorId = 1;\n\nfunction makeCursor(opts) {\n  opts = opts || {};\n  // Translate display-pixel size \u2192 grid-cell brush radius. paintAt\n  // expects grid units; we let the caller think in display px since\n  // that matches the on-screen brush size they're used to from the UI.\n  const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();\n  const pxToGridRadius = (GW / rect.width) * 0.5;\n  // Default size mirrors the smallest in-UI brush (a \"small\" stroke).\n  let size = opts.size !== undefined ? +opts.size : 16;\n  let colorIdx = resolveColorToIndex(opts.color || 'blue');\n  let strength = opts.strength !== undefined ? +opts.strength : 0.35;\n  let x = opts.x !== undefined ? +opts.x : GW * 0.5;\n  let y = opts.y !== undefined ? +opts.y : GH * 0.5;\n\n  const id = apiNextCursorId++;\n\n  const cursor = {\n    get id()       { return id; },\n    get x()        { return x; },\n    get y()        { return y; },\n    get size()     { return size; },\n    get strength() { return strength; },\n    get color() {\n      // Reverse-lookup: return the friendly name for the current index.\n      for (const [name, idx] of Object.entries(API_COLOR_TO_INDEX)) {\n        if (idx === colorIdx) return name;\n      }\n      return 'unknown';\n    },\n\n    // Update position. Coordinates in grid cells, not display pixels \u2014\n    // (0, 0) is top-left, (GW, GH) is bottom-right. Useful for keeping\n    // cursors aligned with sim-internal features (paperH noise, mask\n    // boundaries, etc). To convert display px \u2192 grid: use .gridSize()\n    // utility.\n    move(nx, ny) {\n      if (isFinite(nx)) x = +nx;\n      if (isFinite(ny)) y = +ny;\n      return cursor;\n    },\n\n    // Update appearance properties in one call. Unspecified props keep\n    // their current values. Returns the cursor so calls can chain.\n    set(props) {\n      props = props || {};\n      if (props.color    !== undefined) colorIdx = resolveColorToIndex(props.color);\n      if (props.size     !== undefined) size = +props.size;\n      if (props.strength !== undefined) strength = +props.strength;\n      if (props.x        !== undefined) x = +props.x;\n      if (props.y        !== undefined) y = +props.y;\n      return cursor;\n    },\n\n    // Paint once at the current position with the current properties.\n    // The watercolor sim picks up from there: bleed, edge darkening,\n    // wet diffusion all flow from this single deposit.\n    stamp() {\n      // Recompute pxToGridRadius each call so window resizes are\n      // tracked; the rect's width changes with browser zoom too.\n      const r = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();\n      const g = (GW / r.width) * 0.5;\n      paintAt(x, y, size * g, colorIdx, strength);\n      return cursor;\n    },\n\n    // Detach from the active cursor set. Doesn't undo paint; just lets\n    // the cursor be garbage-collected after the caller drops its\n    // reference. Optional \u2014 cursors that the caller still holds are\n    // cheap (no per-frame cost), and dropping references alone works.\n    remove() {\n      apiCursors.delete(cursor);\n    },\n  };\n  apiCursors.add(cursor);\n  return cursor;\n}\n\n// --- The API surface ----------------------------------------------------\nwindow.Watercolor = {\n  // == Cursors ==========================================================\n  // Create a programmatic cursor. Returns a handle whose methods can be\n  // called to control it. Many cursors can coexist \u2014 the implementation\n  // holds them in a Set for diagnostics, but the caller's references\n  // are what actually keep them alive.\n  cursor: makeCursor,\n  cursors() { return [...apiCursors]; },\n\n  // == Coordinate helpers ===============================================\n  // The canvas has internal \"grid\" coordinates (0..GW \u00d7 0..GH) which is\n  // what cursors and paintAt use. Display coordinates (CSS pixels) are\n  // what mouse events come in as. Helpers convert between them.\n  grid: {\n    get width()  { return GW; },\n    get height() { return GH; },\n  },\n  toGrid(displayX, displayY) {\n    const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();\n    return {\n      x: (displayX - rect.left) * (GW / rect.width),\n      y: (displayY - rect.top)  * (GH / rect.height),\n    };\n  },\n  toDisplay(gridX, gridY) {\n    const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();\n    return {\n      x: gridX * (rect.width / GW)  + rect.left,\n      y: gridY * (rect.height / GH) + rect.top,\n    };\n  },\n\n  // == Animations =======================================================\n  // {replace: true (default)} \u2014 switching mid-stream cleans up the old\n  // preset's in-flight state (no half-fallen rain bleeding into the\n  // new scene). {replace: false} \u2014 additive layering; the previous\n  // preset's particles continue evolving alongside the new one's. The\n  // dispatcher only calls *one* mode's step per frame, so additive\n  // layering is meaningful only for presets that leave persistent\n  // state on the canvas, not for ones that need their own step.\n  setAnimation(name, opts) {\n    opts = opts || {};\n    const replace = opts.replace !== false;\n    if (replace) clearAllAnimationState();\n    animationMode = name || 'off';\n    // Keep the UI dropdown in sync so the user sees the API-driven\n    // change reflected in the controls panel.\n    if (animationSelect) animationSelect.value = animationMode;\n  },\n  getAnimation() { return animationMode; },\n\n  // == Visualizations ===================================================\n  setVisualization(name, opts) {\n    opts = opts || {};\n    const replace = opts.replace !== false;\n    if (replace) clearAllVisualizationState();\n    visualizationMode = name || 'off';\n    if (visualizationSelect) visualizationSelect.value = visualizationMode;\n  },\n  getVisualization() { return visualizationMode; },\n\n  // == Backgrounds ======================================================\n  // Backgrounds are one-shot washes \u2014 the wash runs for ~45 frames,\n  // then nulls itself. replace is accepted but has no effect on a\n  // one-shot, since there's nothing persistent to clear. Calling\n  // setBackground while a wash is already running just overwrites the\n  // preset name; the in-flight wash continues to the new preset's\n  // curves from this frame on.\n  setBackground(name) {\n    startTimeWash(name);\n    if (timeOfDaySelect) timeOfDaySelect.value = name;\n  },\n  // No getBackground \u2014 there's no persistent \"current background\"\n  // state, only an active-wash struct. wasRecently is the closest:\n  isBackgroundRunning() { return timeWash !== null; },\n\n  // == SVG ==============================================================\n  // Trace SVG from a string OR a URL OR a File object.\n  // - String beginning with \"<\" \u2192 treated as SVG markup\n  // - String matching URL pattern \u2192 fetch then trace\n  // - File \u2192 read then trace\n  // Returns the # of sample points queued, or 0 if the input couldn't\n  // be loaded.\n  async traceSVG(source) {\n    let svgText = null;\n    if (source instanceof Blob || (typeof File !== 'undefined' && source instanceof File)) {\n      svgText = await source.text();\n    } else if (typeof source === 'string') {\n      const trimmed = source.trim();\n      if (trimmed.startsWith('<')) {\n        svgText = trimmed;\n      } else {\n        // Looks like a URL/path; try to fetch.\n        try {\n          const resp = await fetch(source);\n          svgText = await resp.text();\n        } catch (e) {\n          console.warn('Watercolor.traceSVG: fetch failed', e);\n          return 0;\n        }\n      }\n    } else {\n      throw new Error('Watercolor.traceSVG: expected string, URL, or File');\n    }\n    return loadSVGAndTrace(svgText);\n  },\n  cancelSVGTrace() { svgTraceState = null; },\n\n  // == Text =============================================================\n  paintText,\n\n  // == Toggles ==========================================================\n  // All toggle setters: pass a boolean to set, omit to read.\n  // Names mirror the on-screen toggle labels for memorability.\n  edgeDarkening(v) {\n    if (v === undefined) return edgeDarkeningEnabled;\n    edgeDarkeningEnabled = !!v;\n    syncToggleUI('toggle-edge', edgeDarkeningEnabled);\n    return Watercolor;\n  },\n  pauseDrying(v) {\n    if (v === undefined) return dryingPaused;\n    dryingPaused = !!v;\n    syncToggleUI('toggle-drying', dryingPaused);\n    return Watercolor;\n  },\n  autoDryBackground(v) {\n    if (v === undefined) return autoDryBackground;\n    autoDryBackground = !!v;\n    syncToggleUI('toggle-autodry', autoDryBackground);\n    return Watercolor;\n  },\n  fadePainting(v) {\n    if (v === undefined) return fadeEnabled;\n    fadeEnabled = !!v;\n    syncToggleUI('toggle-fade', fadeEnabled);\n    return Watercolor;\n  },\n  transparent(v) {\n    if (v === undefined) return transparentBg;\n    transparentBg = !!v;\n    syncToggleUI('toggle-transparent', transparentBg);\n    applyBodyBg();\n    return Watercolor;\n  },\n\n  // == Imperative actions ===============================================\n  // Each maps 1:1 to the matching UI button. Returns the API object for\n  // chaining (e.g. Watercolor.dry().reset().setBackground('night')).\n  rewet()       { rewet();       return Watercolor; },\n  dry()         { dryPaper();    return Watercolor; },\n  removeMask()  { removeMask();  return Watercolor; },\n  exportPNG()   { exportPng();   return Watercolor; },\n  reset()       { resetSim();    return Watercolor; },\n\n  // == Diagnostics =====================================================\n  // Useful when debugging from the console: snapshot the simulation's\n  // top-level state without exposing the giant Float32Arrays directly.\n  state() {\n    return {\n      animation: animationMode,\n      visualization: visualizationMode,\n      backgroundRunning: timeWash !== null,\n      svgTracing: svgTraceState !== null,\n      svgTraceRemaining: svgTraceState\n        ? svgTraceState.points.length - svgTraceState.idx : 0,\n      cursorsActive: apiCursors.size,\n      maskActive,\n      gridWidth: GW,\n      gridHeight: GH,\n      edgeDarkening: edgeDarkeningEnabled,\n      dryingPaused,\n      autoDryBackground,\n      fadePainting: fadeEnabled,\n      transparent: transparentBg,\n    };\n  },\n};\n\n// Helper: keep the visible UI button in sync when the API toggles a\n// setting. Mirrors the click handler's class + aria-pressed updates.\nfunction syncToggleUI(id, isOn) {\n  const el = document.getElementById(id);\n  if (!el) return;\n  el.classList.toggle('on', isOn);\n  el.setAttribute('aria-pressed', String(isOn));\n}\n\n// Surface a one-line console hint on load so curious users discover\n// the API by opening DevTools. The string is intentionally short so it\n// doesn't dominate the console.\nconsole.log(\n  '%cWatercolor API loaded \u2014 try Watercolor.cursor({color:\"rainbow\",size:30}).stamp()',\n  'color:#5a9; font-family:monospace;');\n\n</script>\n</body>\n</html>",

    "text": "**v0.18 \u2014 `window.Watercolor` programmatic API**\n\nOpen DevTools, the console hints at the API on load. Everything in the UI is accessible programmatically:\n\n**Cursors** \u2014 caller-controlled, never auto-paint:\n```js\nconst c = Watercolor.cursor({ color: 'rose', size: 25, x: 100, y: 200, strength: 0.5 });\nc.move(300, 400);                          // grid coordinates\nc.set({ color: 'rainbow', size: 40 });\nc.stamp();                                  // explicit deposit\nc.remove();                                 // detach\n```\n\nColor names: `rose`, `yellow`, `blue` (alias `cerulean`), `rainbow`, `water`, `lift`, `mask`. Caller drives motion \u2014 orbit, follow mouse, choreograph patterns, whatever, by calling `.move()` + `.stamp()` from your own loop.\n\n**Animations / Visualizations** \u2014 per-call replace semantics:\n```js\nWatercolor.setAnimation('tornado');                   // replace = default\nWatercolor.setAnimation('thunderstorm', {replace: false});  // additive\nWatercolor.setVisualization('kaleidoscope');\nWatercolor.setBackground('night');\n```\n\n**SVG** \u2014 accepts markup, URL, or File:\n```js\nawait Watercolor.traceSVG('<svg>\u2026</svg>');\nawait Watercolor.traceSVG('/icons/star.svg');         // fetches\nawait Watercolor.traceSVG(fileFromInputElement);\nWatercolor.cancelSVGTrace();\n```\n\n**Text:**\n```js\nWatercolor.paintText('hello', { fontSize: 120, pigment: 0 });\n```\n\n**Toggles** \u2014 pass bool to set, omit to read:\n```js\nWatercolor.edgeDarkening(false);          // disable\nWatercolor.fadePainting();                // returns current value\nWatercolor.pauseDrying(true);\nWatercolor.autoDryBackground(true);\nWatercolor.transparent(true);\n```\n\n**Imperative actions** \u2014 all chainable:\n```js\nWatercolor.rewet().dry().removeMask().exportPNG().reset();\n```\n\n**Diagnostics:**\n```js\nWatercolor.state();\n// { animation: 'tornado', visualization: 'kaleidoscope', backgroundRunning: false, ... }\nWatercolor.grid;                           // { width: 1152, height: 648 }\nWatercolor.toGrid(mouseX, mouseY);         // event \u2192 grid coords\n```\n\nUI stays in sync \u2014 calling `Watercolor.setAnimation('tornado')` updates the dropdown so users can see what's happening from API calls.\n\n**Try this in the console** for a six-cursor radial paint:\n```js\nWatercolor.reset();\nconst cs = Array.from({length:6}, (_,i) => Watercolor.cursor({color: ['rose','yellow','blue'][i%3], size: 18}));\nlet t = 0;\nsetInterval(() => {\n  t += 0.03;\n  const cx = Watercolor.grid.width/2, cy = Watercolor.grid.height/2;\n  cs.forEach((c, i) => {\n    const angle = t + i * Math.PI/3;\n    const r = 200 + Math.sin(t*2) * 80;\n    c.move(cx + Math.cos(angle)*r, cy + Math.sin(angle)*r).stamp();\n  });\n}, 33);\n```\n\nThat's a 6-cursor kaleidoscope coded from scratch in 8 lines, using just the API.",

    "type": "text",
    "text": " That's a tough one to answer without measurement \u2014 I don't actually know what you're seeing. A few honest possibilities, ordered roughly by how likely they are:\n\n**1. JavaScriptCore vs V8 on this specific workload.** Safari uses JavaScriptCore; Chrome uses V8. The hot path here is *tight numeric loops over Float32Arrays* \u2014 `diffuseWet`, `movePigment`, `transferPigment`, `render`. V8's TurboFan tends to be more aggressive at unboxing floats and inlining typed-array reads in those exact patterns. JSC is competitive on most code but historically a bit behind on dense numeric loops with branching. This isn't universal \u2014 JSC wins on plenty of workloads \u2014 but for this code it could plausibly favor V8.\n\n**2. `putImageData` / `drawImage` pipeline differences.** The render does a `putImageData` to an offscreen canvas, then `drawImage` it scaled up to the display canvas. Safari and Chrome have different GPU upload paths for this. On retina displays especially, Safari sometimes does extra color-space conversions (P3 vs sRGB) that Chrome skips by default.\n\n**3. Throttling.** Safari throttles `requestAnimationFrame` more aggressively when the tab isn't focused, when battery is low, or under thermal pressure. If \"Claude's browser\" is a fresh Chromium window in the foreground and Safari was backgrounded or had many tabs open, that alone could explain the gap.\n\n**4. Hardware acceleration / Low Power Mode.** Safari on macOS respects Low Power Mode harder than Chrome does. If your laptop is on battery and Low Power is on, Safari will down-clock animation work.\n\n**5. It might not actually be faster \u2014 just smoother.** Different `rAF` cadences feel different even at the same effective FPS. Chrome's frame pacing is generally tighter.\n\nIf you want to actually know, the cheapest check is open both side-by-side, hit the same preset (try **Background: Night + Auto-dry off + Visualization: Kaleidoscope** \u2014 that's the heaviest combo), and watch the dryness/wetness bars. They update at 10 Hz from real frame counts and they'll diverge if one browser is doing fewer sim steps per second. I could also add an FPS readout to the panel if you want to measure rather than guess \u2014 say the word.",

    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-11T14:59:45.658627Z",

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": " Yes \u2014 FPS alone is a blunt instrument. Several more revealing metrics for a workload like this:\n\n**Frame time (ms/frame) with percentiles.** Average FPS hides jank. A canvas hitting 60 fps average but with 2% of frames at 80ms feels stuttery; one at 50 fps with tight variance feels smooth. Track p50, p95, p99 frame times in a ring buffer. The gap between p50 and p99 *is* the jank signal.\n\n**Per-phase breakdown.** The frame budget here is spent in clearly separable steps: `applyPendingPaint`, `animationStep`, `visualizationStep`, `svgTraceStep`, `simStep` (\u00d72 when active), `fadeStep`, `timeWashStep`, `render`. Time each with `performance.now()` deltas and you can see which phase is eating budget. Usually it's `render` (full pixel sweep) or `movePigment` (the advection + diffusion loops). Without this you're guessing.\n\n**Active cell count.** This codebase tracks an active rectangle but the actual \"live\" cell count varies wildly \u2014 a fresh canvas with one dab might have 200 live cells; a full Night-wash + Tornado might have 700,000. Cost scales linearly with this. Plotting it alongside frame time tells you whether slowdowns are algorithmic or just the canvas being wetter.\n\n**Allocations per frame.** Garbage collection pauses are a major source of jank. Chrome's `performance.memory.usedJSHeapSize` (non-standard, Chromium-only) sampled per frame shows the sawtooth pattern of allocations + GC. If the sawtooth is sharp, you have an allocation hot spot. The kaleidoscope visualization, for instance, allocates a `getBoundingClientRect()` object every frame \u2014 small but visible.\n\n**Long Animation Frames (LoAF).** Newer Performance Observer API exposes frames that blocked the main thread longer than 50ms with attribution to scripts. `new PerformanceObserver(list => ...)` with `entryTypes: ['long-animation-frame']`. Much better than FPS for catching real user-perceptible jank.\n\n**GPU vs CPU time.** Chrome DevTools' Performance tab shows compositor + raster work separately from main-thread JS. For this canvas, JS dominates, but on a transparent body + drawImage upscale, the compositor sometimes shows up as a meaningful chunk.\n\n**Input latency.** Time from `pointermove` event to next paint that reflects it. The pendingPaint queue in this code accumulates per-event coordinates and flushes once per frame, so latency is bounded at ~1 frame + sim cost. Measure it directly by timestamping the event and subtracting at the next `render` call.\n\n**Visual stability (CLS analog).** Less relevant here than for typical pages, but worth knowing: layout shifts in chrome elements could be measured if you cared.\n\nFor this specific tool the highest-value additions would be **(1) p99 frame time**, **(2) per-phase ms breakdown**, and **(3) active cell count**. Together they tell you not just \"is it slow\" but \"what is slow and why.\" A small overlay readout would be cheap \u2014 maybe 15 lines of code, gated behind a `?perf=1` URL flag so it doesn't bother regular users.\n\nWant me to add that as an option in v0.19?",
